title: Traveling with Kyle - Living in Chile Part 15/17

transcript.text: I was like, I felt all this electricity pass through my entire body, man.
And I thought I was going to die, but I was like, it totally charged me when I let go.
And I was like stuck. I couldn't let go.
But when I managed to let go, I was like charged. I was like angry.
I was like, what the fuck? I don't want to fucking die yet.
What the fuck are you doing?
So I went and I put the tape over it.
And, you know, I was just careful, man, after that.
But I mean, yeah, like my wife's, the father of my wife's son, his brother died.
He stepped out of a bathtub after taking a shower.
And he had a live wire running through the floor of his tub.
And he stepped on it and fucking killed him.
Because I guess he was all wet.
And he was only like 23 years old.
But, um, so, uh, yeah.
But, yeah, man.
Um, lots of weird shit happened in that neighborhood.
Like, like there is this two, on two doors down, there's this guy who lived right next to us.
He was this gay guy who I actually never even saw once.
Um, I guess he was locked up in his house all the time.
But, uh, he, uh, and I looked in his backyard and it was totally like dilapidated.
I mean, our house wasn't the only house that was totally dilapidated.
All the houses there was.
Except for the house across the street.
Where her childhood friend was from.
They had a cool backyard.
Oh, and her best friend.
They had a cool backyard, too.
But, like, when I looked in the house behind us.
And both of the houses behind us and the one next to us.
All the whole backyard was just overgrown grass.
And just bricks and, like, pieces of wood piled everywhere.
Festering ground for rats.
And I guess they do, they put rat poison all over the place.
Which is bad because it kills cats.
Like, we had a cool cat named Plomo.
Named Plomi.
Which means silver.
Because he was a silver cat.
He wasn't even full grown yet.
He just disappeared one day.
And we figured he died from eating poison.
And, uh, yeah.
And, like, their family dog died a couple weeks after we moved back to America.
And they didn't know why.
And maybe that was because he got into some poison.
But, um.
But, yeah.
But, like, so there's this gay guy.
And then, and then right on the other side of the gay guy, two houses down from us, was this old lady.
And then her daughter, who was like my wife's age.
And then she had a son who was like 11.
And, you know, you see that a lot.
Like three generations living in one house.
I mean, we had three generations living in our house.
And there were even four generations in the family.
My wife's father's mother lived out on the other side of town.
And she'd come over a couple times.
But, um, yeah.
But I guess, and so, like, when they don't get along with each other, they still, they don't have the money to move out.
So they have to stay living with each other.
And so that's kind of what caused a lot of problems with the family between my wife's mom and dad.
Because they, like, really should have been getting divorced.
But they, uh, they were just, they were together because they just had to out of necessity living in the same house.
Um, it's interesting how they got that house.
They, uh, they, they, they bought the house.
And they were paying the mortgage.
And they still owed, like, I don't know how much, all this mortgage on it.
But then the coup happened in 1973.
And everybody in the country took their money out of the bank, except for a couple of people, like my wife's parents, probably because they were just too lazy to do it.
But they didn't do it.
And, uh, and so, like, as a reward to them, Pinochet bought the rest of their house for them.
Just paid it off.
So, they, they totally got hooked up.
But, um, yeah, so, like, two houses down, this lady, there's this old lady, and I think I remember seeing her.
I guess she'd be up really early every morning, like, sweeping.
But her daughter, like, freaked out one day.
Like, she suddenly disappeared.
And everybody thought that, and she told all of her family members that her mother had moved down south to, to her other family members in Patagonia.
But, like, those family members said that she never came there and she didn't know what happened to her.
So, they kind of started to suspect foul play.
So, the cops came over.

And, anyway, what happened, what turned out happening was she banged her mama over the head with a brick, with a hammer, killed her, made her son, her 11-year-old son, dismember the body and put it into a, uh, back into a suitcase and bury it in the backyard only four inches underground.
And so, the cops came one night and they, uh, they exhumed it.
And, uh, I remember seeing him taking the body out on the stretcher into the, uh, into the, the, uh, ambulance.
And they had, like, a bunch of fire trucks there or something.
And they had, like, the national news come.
And they actually interviewed my wife.
And so, I saw my wife on national news getting interviewed for, like, a long time.
I'm just, like, talking about the lady who did it because they were, like, childhood friends and, like, oh, she seemed normal to me.
I don't know.
And I remember seeing, like, my wife, she was really hot on TV.
And then they interviewed the lady who this woman lived with.
I guess this woman, she got creeped out by living in her house after she killed her mom.
So, she moved out of her house and just left the house there.
Just, like, abandoned and moved into some other, like, cheap hostel.
And so, they interviewed the, the neighbor of her, of the people of her cheap hostel.
And I recognized that girl who they named, who they interviewed.
She was the girlfriend of my drug dealer who I got weed from.
So, that was cool.
That was crazy.
So, and I guess a lot of people saw me also in commercials that I made.
And I saw my wife's nephew, the kid who played with me, I saw him in some commercials.
But I never once saw myself in a commercial.
And I never bothered to get a copy of the commercials that I did, man.
I wish I did.
Put them on YouTube.
Maybe I could call the company, find out who they are and call them and, and, and get, get my hands on that commercial.
That'd be cool.
But, anyways, because I did one commercial where I was, like, this businessman walking down the road.
And I'm, like, supposed to pretend that I'm, like, really, like, tired, really hot.
Because, like, I have the wrong kinds of shoes on.
And I'm, like, oh, oh, oh.
I was, like, the grumpy businessman.
And they had the other businessman who was, like, who was, like, the happy businessman with the good shoes.
I was always typecasted as the businessman because I was a white and stuff.
But, anyways, yeah.
So, they exhumed her and I smelled it, man.
And it smelled, like, you know, like skunk or something.

And, yeah, they even went in our backyard and stood on top of this big semi that I guess her dad was fixing to film the backyard to get time to get up a shot of where they exhumed the body and everything.
And then, a couple months later, they got some new tenants in there.
And, uh, in that same exact house, something happened.
And that house, like, burned down.
There were these flames that were, like, 30 feet high.
And so, these two, uh, fire trucks come and put the fire out.
And then, and then national news comes again.
And they come to our backyard, jump on that truck again, and film the fire from the same exact place that they filmed the exhumation from just a couple months beforehand.
So, it's, like, we were, like, the center for, like, national news.
That's what they'd come over and film from our backyard because of this cursed house.
But, yeah, it was kind of a rough neighborhood.
Like, there was this eight-year-old kid who got raped and murdered only four blocks from our house.
And, um, you know, my son, stepson got mugged coming home from the subway, which just opened a couple months before we left.
And I actually got mugged, too, of my bike.
Only three weeks before I came home, I went down there on my sandals to the projects to get weed.
And I usually, when I went down there, I'd go down there with my running shoes just in case someone tried to mug me.
I could fight them off.
But I went down there that time with these really bad sandals that would fall off if I tried to run.
And I just got bum-rushed, man.
I just, there was just all these kids around me, you know, punching me in the face and, like, yanking on my bike and picking my pockets and just got overwhelmed by the numbers.
And I couldn't fight him because I didn't want to get my feet stomped and get these blisters on my feet and stuff.
So, I ran after the kid, but he ran off and, and, uh, that was it for my bike.
It was my fault, though.

Because I knew that these kids wanted to rob me because they were, they'd always, like, give me the evil eye and, uh, you know, run up to me and be like, like, you know, give me money or I'll get you, I'll get you weed.
And I was like, what?
You give you money and then you come back?
That's not going to work.
And then he'd, like, roll his eyes and, like, scowl at me and walk off and, but he didn't fuck with me because I had my shoes on, but just that one time.
And I was also, like, really high when I went down there and, like, it was, like, three weeks before I came back and just didn't have any energy.
It was stupid.
But I guess if something like that has to happen once in your life.
But, uh, yeah, I gave, I gave, like, ten bucks to one old guy there to get me some weed and he just disappeared.
But, uh, yeah.
But it was cool to be able to get weed whenever I wanted, only it was really bad weed.
It was, like, a buck and a half to get a little bit, but it was, like, it was, like, brick from Paraguay.
And once when I got high with my stepson when he was 13, he started smoking weed.
I mean, I was smoking weed in front of him since he was nine, but he never wanted to smoke weed with me when he ended up getting high with me.
And actually, he got high with me before he got high with his own dad.
He started smoking weed with his dad after he started smoking weed.
But the first time, he's, like, yo, you want to get high?
I was, like, did you smoke weed?
He's, like, I got some.
I was, like, what?
So we're getting high and he busts out his Paraguayan weed and he holds the brick up to me.
He's, like, oh, look, a bug.
And I was, like, what?
We look at it and there's this, like, petrified fucking mosquito in there.
And he's, like, oh, look, yeah, a piece of honey.
And he pulls out a piece of honey and shows it to me.
I was, like, oh, my God, those bastards, they mix, I mean, they mix.
